question: Q1,Q2,Q3
title: jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 - Critically Outdated with Known CVEs (Current: 9.x)
data_flow: JWT tokens created/verified via jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 → used throughout auth middleware (express-jwt) → bearer tokens in Authorization headers → user identity/session for all protected routes
exploit_scenario: jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 is 10+ major versions behind current 9.x. CVE-2022-23529: object injection via secretOrPublicKey. CVE-2022-23540: accepts 'none' algorithm allowing unsigned tokens. CVE-2022-23541: algorithm confusion attacks (RS256 vs HS256 key confusion). Attacker forges arbitrary JWT tokens claiming admin identity by passing alg:none in the header, bypassing signature verification entirely.
framework_protection: None — algorithm enforcement was only added in jsonwebtoken 6.x. Version 0.4.0 predates all security hardening. The alg:none bypass is trivially exploitable.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to jsonwebtoken ^9.0.0. Explicitly whitelist allowed algorithms via the algorithms option in jwt.verify(). Rotate all secrets and invalidate existing sessions after upgrade.

question: Q1,Q3
title: express-jwt 0.1.3 - Pre-Security-Fix Version with No Algorithm Validation (Current: 8.x)
data_flow: express-jwt middleware validates JWT on incoming HTTP requests → passes decoded payload as req.user → downstream route handlers trust req.user for authorization decisions
exploit_scenario: express-jwt 0.1.3 is approximately 17 major versions behind current 8.x. This version does not enforce algorithm checking and does not handle algorithm confusion, potentially passing malformed or unsigned tokens through as valid. Combined with jsonwebtoken 0.4.0, an attacker can craft a JWT with alg:none and arbitrary claims including admin role, then gain full admin access without valid credentials. The devDependency @types/express-jwt references ^6.0.4 — a different version than what is installed — confirming known version mismatch.
framework_protection: None — algorithm validation was added in express-jwt 5.x.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to express-jwt ^8.0.0. Set the algorithms option explicitly in middleware configuration. Audit all routes using req.user for authorization bypass vulnerabilities.

question: Q1,Q3
title: sanitize-html 1.4.2 - XSS Bypass via Outdated HTML Parser (Current: 2.x)
data_flow: User-supplied HTML input → sanitize-html 1.4.2 → stored in database or rendered to page → reflected back to other users in product reviews and feedback forms
exploit_scenario: sanitize-html 1.4.2 (released circa 2016) has documented XSS bypass vectors fixed in subsequent versions. CVE-2021-26540 (1.x series) allows bypass via crafted tag attributes. Multiple parser confusion issues allow javascript: URIs in href and src attributes to survive sanitization. Attacker posts a review containing crafted HTML with a malicious URI that the sanitizer fails to strip. When another user views the review, the XSS payload executes in their browser, stealing session tokens or performing actions on their behalf.
framework_protection: Angular's template engine provides some protection for Angular-rendered content, but sanitize-html is used server-side for content stored in DB and served via API. Server-side sanitization failures bypass Angular's client-side DomSanitizer.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to sanitize-html ^2.13.0. Review and tighten allowedTags and allowedAttributes allowlists. Test with known bypass payloads from OWASP XSS cheat sheet after upgrade.

question: Q1,Q3
title: libxmljs2 ~0.37.0 - XXE Vulnerability via Default External Entity Processing
data_flow: User uploads XML file or provides XML input → libxmljs2 parses XML with default settings → external entity expansion enabled by default → file system read or SSRF possible via XXE
exploit_scenario: libxmljs2 uses libxml2 under the hood with external entity processing enabled by default. An attacker submits XML containing a DOCTYPE with a SYSTEM entity referencing local files (file:///etc/passwd) or internal HTTP endpoints. The parser resolves the entity, reading local files or making server-side HTTP requests. SSRF can be escalated to access cloud metadata endpoints to steal IAM credentials. The tilde range ~0.37.0 locks to patch versions of 0.37.x only, blocking automatic security patches from 0.38+.
framework_protection: None in libxmljs2 0.37.x — external entities must be explicitly disabled by passing options to parseXml().
fix_recommendation: Pass { noent: false, dtdload: false, dtdvalid: false } to all libxmljs2.parseXml() calls. Upgrade to the latest libxmljs2 version. Consider replacing with fast-xml-parser with processEntities disabled.

question: Q2,Q5
title: unzipper 0.9.15 - Outdated with Zip Slip (Path Traversal) History (Current: 0.12.x)
data_flow: User uploads ZIP file → unzipper 0.9.15 extracts archive entries → entries written to filesystem → path traversal via crafted entry names can write outside the intended extraction directory
exploit_scenario: unzipper 0.9.15 is multiple minor versions behind current 0.12.x. Older unzipper versions are vulnerable to Zip Slip — path traversal via ../ sequences in ZIP entry filenames. An attacker uploads a ZIP archive containing an entry with a path-traversal filename targeting a sensitive system location. When extracted, the file is written outside the intended directory, potentially overwriting critical system or application files to enable unauthorized access or code execution. The fixed version 0.9.15 (not a caret range) means no automatic updates ever occur.
framework_protection: No path sanitization is applied to extracted filenames before writing in unzipper 0.9.x.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to unzipper ^0.12.0. Add explicit path traversal validation before writing each entry: verify that path.resolve(destDir, entry.path) starts with path.resolve(destDir). Consider adm-zip with explicit path validation.

question: Q2,Q5
title: helmet 4.6.0 - Two Major Versions Behind, Missing Modern Isolation Headers (Current: 8.x)
data_flow: helmet middleware sets HTTP security headers on all responses → browser enforces Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and other headers → older helmet omits headers added in v5 through v8
exploit_scenario: helmet 4.x does not include Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy (COOP), Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy (COEP), or Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy (CORP) headers added in helmet 5+. Missing COOP allows cross-origin window handle access, enabling XS-Leaks side-channel attacks. helmet 4 also does not set Content-Security-Policy by default (opt-in only), meaning that if the application does not explicitly configure CSP, there is no browser-level XSS mitigation.
framework_protection: helmet 4.x still sets X-XSS-Protection, X-Content-Type-Options, and X-Frame-Options. Core protections are present but modern isolation headers are absent.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to helmet ^8.0.0. Enable and configure CSP explicitly with a strict policy. Run integration tests to verify new header defaults do not break existing functionality.

question: Q1,Q2
title: socket.io 3.1.2 - Outdated, engine.io DoS CVE Present (Current: 4.x)
data_flow: socket.io handles WebSocket connections for chat and notification features → attacker controls WebSocket handshake and message payloads → underlying engine.io parsing can be targeted for DoS
exploit_scenario: socket.io 3.x is based on engine.io 4.x which contains CVE-2022-21676: Denial of Service via crafted WebSocket handshake data. An attacker sends malformed WebSocket frames that trigger resource exhaustion in engine.io parsing, causing server crashes or excessive memory/CPU consumption that takes the application offline. socket.io 4.x (current) resolved multiple issues in engine.io and the adapter layer. The devDependency socket.io-client is also at 3.1.3, matching the outdated server version.
framework_protection: socket.io 3.x has some input validation, but the DoS in underlying engine.io affects all 3.x versions prior to patched releases.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to socket.io ^4.8.0. Update socket.io-client from ^3.1.3 to ^4.8.0 to maintain version compatibility.

question: Q2,Q5
title: node-pre-gyp 0.15.0 in Production Dependencies - Deprecated Unmaintained Package
data_flow: node-pre-gyp downloads pre-built native binaries during npm install → fetches from remote URLs using vulnerable node-fetch → placed in production dependencies unnecessarily, expanding production attack surface
exploit_scenario: The original node-pre-gyp package is deprecated and unmaintained — the project was forked to @mapbox/node-pre-gyp. Version 0.15.0 uses node-fetch 2.x with CVE-2022-0235 (open redirect). In build environments, a man-in-the-middle on the binary download URL or a compromised package registry could redirect binary downloads to attacker-controlled binaries. Additionally, the package is in production dependencies rather than devDependencies, meaning it is present in production containers where it serves no purpose.
framework_protection: npm install integrity checks (with lockfile SRI hashes) provide partial mitigation for supply chain attacks on binary downloads.
fix_recommendation: Replace with @mapbox/node-pre-gyp (the maintained fork). Move to devDependencies since it is only needed at build time for native module compilation. Ensure package-lock.json is committed and npm ci is used in production builds.

question: Q4
title: Build Tools in Production Dependencies: grunt, ts-node-dev Inflate Attack Surface
data_flow: Standard npm install (without --omit=dev flag) installs grunt, ts-node-dev, replace, and grunt plugins as production dependencies → production container surface area expanded → TypeScript compilation and task automation tools active in production environment
exploit_scenario: grunt, ts-node-dev, replace, grunt-contrib-compress, and grunt-replace-json are development and build tools with no legitimate production runtime purpose. Their presence in production: ts-node-dev enables live TypeScript compilation — if upload paths are misconfigured, injected TypeScript files could be compiled and executed at runtime; grunt's plugin loading system can be abused if Gruntfile.js is writable; the replace utility performs file content substitution that could be misused. The package:ci script calls npm prune --production, but standard Docker builds using plain npm install do not prune.
framework_protection: The package:ci script does call npm prune --production, mitigating this when the CI packaging path is used. Risk is conditional on Docker build methodology.
fix_recommendation: Move grunt, ts-node-dev, replace, grunt-contrib-compress, and grunt-replace-json to devDependencies. Verify all production Docker builds use npm ci --omit=dev or explicitly call npm prune --production.

question: Q2,Q5
title: multer 1.4.5-lts.1 - End-of-Life 1.x Branch, Superseded by 2.x with Security Fixes
data_flow: multer handles multipart/form-data file uploads → parses MIME boundaries and file content → stores temporary files on disk → application processes uploaded files from challenge endpoints
exploit_scenario: multer 1.4.5-lts.1 is an LTS security backport indicating the 1.x branch has reached end-of-life. multer 2.x (current) includes fixes for potential ReDoS in boundary parsing, improved file type validation, and fixes for multipart header injection issues. The range ^1.4.5-lts.1 will never resolve to 2.x. Crafted malformed multipart requests targeting 1.x boundary parsing bugs can trigger denial of service or potentially bypass file type checks.
framework_protection: Juice Shop applies separate file-type checking using the file-type package, providing partial mitigation against content-type spoofing attacks.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to multer ^2.0.0. Test all upload endpoints after upgrade for breaking API changes in file handling callback signatures.

question: Q6
title: Caret Version Ranges on Security-Critical Dependencies Allow Silent Vulnerable Version Pulls
data_flow: npm install resolves caret ranges to latest compatible minor and patch versions → without an enforced lockfile via npm ci, fresh clones can pull unpinned versions → supply chain or regression vulnerabilities enter silently without any manifest change
exploit_scenario: The caret (^) range allows any minor and patch update within the same major version. Without a committed package-lock.json enforced via npm ci in all build contexts, a fresh npm install months from now could pull a newly vulnerable patch version. pug has a history of SSTI vulnerabilities and caret ranges do not prevent pulling newly introduced issues in pug 3.x. sequelize ^6.37.3 allows pulling any 6.x version including potential future regressions. This supply-chain hygiene issue compounds all other component vulnerabilities.
framework_protection: If package-lock.json is committed and npm ci is used in all builds, this is mitigated for installs from the existing lockfile. However npm install (not npm ci) still resolves ranges dynamically and can produce different results.
fix_recommendation: Commit package-lock.json to version control. Use npm ci in all CI/CD pipelines and Docker builds instead of npm install. Consider pinning critical security dependencies (jsonwebtoken, express-jwt, sequelize) to exact versions.

question: Q3
title: js-yaml 3.14.0 - Dangerous load() Function Present, Fixed Only in 4.x
data_flow: js-yaml parses YAML configuration files or user-supplied YAML input → parsed objects used in application logic → the load() function in 3.x can execute arbitrary JavaScript when given untrusted YAML with JS-specific tags
exploit_scenario: js-yaml 3.x exposes yaml.load() which, unlike safeLoad(), permits YAML documents using JavaScript-specific tags that invoke arbitrary JavaScript at parse time. In js-yaml 4.x, load() was redesigned to be safe by default and safeLoad() was removed to eliminate the naming confusion. If any Juice Shop code uses js-yaml's load() on user-controlled input (such as uploaded YAML files in challenge scenarios), an attacker can supply YAML using JS function type tags to achieve remote code execution on the server. The ^3.14.0 range will never pull js-yaml 4.x where this vulnerability class is eliminated by design.
framework_protection: If all call sites use yaml.safeLoad() rather than yaml.load(), the risk is mitigated in 3.x — but this requires a code-level audit to confirm consistently across the codebase.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to js-yaml ^4.1.0 where load() is safe by default. Audit all require('js-yaml').load( call sites to confirm no untrusted input is passed. Search codebase for yaml.load( to identify risky usages.

question: Q3
title: pug 3.0.3 - Server-Side Template Injection Risk if User Input Reaches Template Position
data_flow: pug renders server-side HTML templates → if user input is concatenated into template strings rather than passed as template data variables → SSTI allows arbitrary server-side code execution
exploit_scenario: pug (formerly jade) is vulnerable to Server-Side Template Injection if user-controlled strings are passed directly to pug.compile() or pug.render() as the template source argument rather than as template data variables. If any Juice Shop route constructs a pug template by concatenating user input, an attacker can submit a payload using pug interpolation syntax that references Node.js globals to read sensitive files or execute operating system commands, achieving remote code execution. Even with correct data passing, older pug versions had unescaped interpolation leading to XSS in specific rendering contexts.
framework_protection: If user input is only passed as data (the second argument to pug.render(template, data)) and never as the template string itself, SSTI is not possible. This requires code-level verification of all render call sites.
fix_recommendation: Audit all pug.render(), pug.compile(), and res.render() calls to confirm user input is never in the template source position. Upgrade to the latest pug 3.x patch. Add strict input validation middleware for routes using dynamic template rendering.

question: Q4
title: errorhandler 1.5.1 in Production Dependencies - Dev-Only Middleware Exposing Stack Traces
data_flow: errorhandler Express middleware intercepts unhandled errors → formats full stack traces including local variables → sends detailed internal error information in HTTP responses → production errors expose internal paths, source structure, and server configuration
exploit_scenario: errorhandler is explicitly designed for development use only — its own documentation warns against production use. When active in production, unhandled Express errors return full stack traces in HTTP responses, revealing: server file system paths, Node.js module structure, source code line snippets, database query strings if the error originated from the ORM, and internal network addresses. An attacker deliberately triggers error conditions (malformed input, invalid resource identifiers, boundary values) and reads the detailed stack trace to fingerprint the application stack, identify file paths, and discover attack vectors for further exploitation.
framework_protection: Express has a built-in production mode (NODE_ENV=production) that suppresses some error detail, but errorhandler overrides this suppression when registered as middleware.
fix_recommendation: Remove errorhandler from production dependencies or guard its registration with an environment check: register it only when NODE_ENV equals 'development'. Move to devDependencies. Replace with a production-safe error handler that logs internally and returns only a generic error message to clients.

question: Q2
title: fs-extra 9.1.0 - Three Major Versions Behind Current Stable (11.x)
data_flow: fs-extra provides file system utilities used for copying challenge files, reading user uploads, and managing runtime directories throughout the application
exploit_scenario: fs-extra 9.x is three major versions behind current stable 11.x. While no critical CVEs are directly attributed to fs-extra independently, outdated file system manipulation libraries in security-sensitive code paths (file upload handling, challenge verification) can interact with and amplify other vulnerabilities. The ^9.1.0 range blocks migration to improved atomic write operations and symlink handling behavior fixes introduced in versions 10.x and 11.x.
framework_protection: fs-extra is a thin wrapper over the Node.js built-in fs module, limiting its independent vulnerability surface area.
fix_recommendation: Upgrade to fs-extra ^11.0.0. Check for breaking API changes in move() and copy() method signatures and behavior.
